Cost of Deposits

1.97%

2.18%

Most of the metrics listed above are also trending in the right direction. Net interest margin improved 34 basis points in 2025. The cost of deposits for the bank fell by 51 basis points in the last year. The efficiency ratio improved from 53.58% at the end of 2024 to its most current level of 50.32%. In other words, the operations of the bank appear to be getting stronger.

There is one exception to this observation. Return on equity deteriorated slightly over the last 12 months. ROE fell from 11.61% at the end of 2024 to 10.61% at the end of 2025. This is below the national average for a bank of BY’s size, which is 12.04%.

As mentioned earlier, deposits at Byline Bank actually fell in the most recent quarter, from about $7.8 billion to $7.6 billion. The bank is able to continue to keep its cost of deposits low, in part, because of its share of non-interest-bearing deposits, which represented almost 24% of the total composition as shown below. The bank also was able to decrease its cost of interest-bearing deposits 24 basis points in Q4 to 2.61%. The national average for this metric is 2.81%. However, the bank will need to regain its momentum for deposit growth in the year ahead, especially if its loan portfolio is going to continue to expand at a rapid clip.

One last data point worth mentioning for Byline Bancorp is its book value, which made a significant move upward in 2025. After starting the year at $24.55 per share, it ended at $27.84. This is a 13.4% improvement. Tangible book value rose by an even larger share. Its move from $20.09 to $23.44 in the last 12 months is an increase of 16.7%. BY currently trades at a reasonable price-to-TBV ratio of 1.35.

Risks to Consider

Asset quality trends for Byline Bank can be seen in the snapshot below. The bank has been consistently above the national average for net charge-offs for the last four quarters. This has not elevated my concern in the past because there were so many other strong qualities in the bank’s operations, but some of this additional data is starting to wave a small warning flag in my head. The ratio of non-performing loans was on a gradual upward swing in 2025, finishing at its highest point of the year at 0.95%. Allowances for credit losses have remained relatively consistent through the last year. However, criticized and classified loans and leases increased 50 basis points from Q3 and 110 basis points from Q4 2024. The bank carries a significant share of commercial loans that are on the higher end of the risk spectrum, mainly industrial loans and investor CRE.

Byline Bancorp has projected that the bank will cross $10 billion in assets as early as Q1 2026. Once this takes place, Byline Bank will be subject to new rules and regulations that could make future operations more challenging. The bank will be under the direct supervision of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau for regulatory compliance. Non-interest expenses may increase due to the need for added oversight and governance.

The limited geographic footprint for Byline Bank also adds risks in the event of economic challenges that impact the Chicago area. Political instability, rising unemployment, or changes in economic policy with regard to taxes or business regulation could provide headwinds for business activity in the area in 2026. The state of Illinois is facing challenges related to increased government spending that has caused a need for greater tax revenue. Yet, higher taxes may dampen economic activity in the state’s largest city.

The Bottom Line

Seeking Alpha’s Quant analysis seems to agree with my own, as BY is considered a Hold. Its lowest two sub-scores are in Valuation and Growth, two areas where I prefer not to see below average marks. The company’s substandard increase in earnings along with its declining return on equity played significant roles in BY’s “D+” in Growth.

Despite the concerns mentioned here, there is still a lot to like about Byline Bancorp. Many of its profitability metrics are still above the national average, and the growth in tangible book value over the last 12 months cannot be ignored. However, the slow growth in the company’s bottom line numbers and the concerns mentioned with regard to the bank’s loan portfolio are enough to move my opinion of the stock from a Buy to a Hold.

data.title: Ericsson's Jump Was Well Deserved
data.description:
January 23rd was a pretty good day for shareholders of Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (publ) (ERIC), or Ericsson for short. Shares of the company jumped 8.9% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue and earnings per share both exceeded what analysts expected them to. And even though financial performance was worse on the top line year over year, most bottom line results showed improvement. Long term, this is a company that I believe has potential. That is why, back in April of last year, I called it a ‘buy.’ Since then, the stock has done incredibly well, rising 33.1% compared to the 29.6% increase that the S&P 500 enjoyed.

Another great thing about the company is that, even as it continues to reinvent itself and focus on key growth opportunities, is the fact that shares remain attractively priced. The business has a rock-solid balance sheet, and that is also deserving of attention. Given this combination of factors, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ makes all the sense in the world at this point in time.

A necessary disclosure

Just like I did in my previous article about Ericsson, I have to point out a very important fact about this particular article. You see, Ericsson is a US-based firm. In fact, it is incorporated in Sweden. This means that its financial results are reported to investors in Swedish krona. I personally think in dollar terms, and I would imagine that I have a lot more US-based readers than I do Swedish ones. So what I did in this article is convert all financial figures into U.S. dollars based on the current exchange rate. Having said that, in any of the images provided by management, all financial results shown are in the company's native currency.

A great quarter

Relative to what analysts were expecting, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a great time for shareholders of Ericsson. The company generated revenue of $7.74 billion. This was unfortunately below the $8.15 billion the company reported a year earlier. But as disappointing as this was, it did at least represent outperformance compared to what analysts anticipated of $251 million. The data management report meant that overall revenue for 2025 in its entirety came in at $26.44 billion. That's down from the $27.70 billion reported a year earlier. This means that the drop in revenue experienced during the final quarter was not a blip on the radar. It's part of a more recent trend for the business.

Profitability is a different animal entirely. Earnings per share for the business shot up from $0.16 to $0.28 on a year-over-year basis. This reading ended up exceeding analysts’ expectations by $0.04 and meant a surge in net profits from $545 million to $958 million. That's a huge increase during such a short window of time. Most other profitability metrics improved also. The one exception to this was operating cash flow, which contracted from $1.96 billion to $1.84 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise to $1.43 billion from $1.25 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $1.24 billion to $1.45 billion. As the chart above illustrates, profitability for the year in its entirety was higher for 2025 with the exception of operating cash flow. So even though revenue for the company is contracting, management is getting better at capturing more of that revenue in the form of profits for shareholders.

Digging in deeper, we find that the revenue decline that the company exhibited was driven by two of its three major operating segments. The largest of these is its Networks segment, which is engaged in the provision of hardware and software solutions and related service offerings to help communications services providers as they continue to work on rolling out 5G. Specific offerings that this unit provides include, but are not limited to, RAN (Radio Access Network) products like antennas, radios, software, and the like. It's also responsible for the production of RAN units that are used for computational purposes. Some of the technology that the company makes available for customers consists of fiber and microwave connections that help to connect RAN nodes in order to facilitate 5G mobile networks. And it provides various support services associated with all of this.

Revenue for the final quarter associated with the Networks segment amounted to $4.94 billion. That was unfortunately a drop from the $5.23 billion reported the previous year. Overall organic sales seem to have been flat. The company did, however, see certain licensing revenues decline. The big weakness for the company, however, ended up being its foreign currency fluctuations.

As a truly global company, with operations spread across several countries, including those throughout the Americas, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and major East Asian markets such as India, exposure to foreign currency can have a big impact, sometimes for the better and sometimes for the worst, on financial performance. According to management, however, without this, revenue would have risen by about 4%. And this is because the company benefited from sales growth in certain markets.

The Middle East and Africa were particularly promising, while in Vietnam the firm saw robust performance. The growth in these markets seems to be the result of continuing network modernization and 5G launches. Projects underway in various countries, with examples being Saudi Arabia, Algeria, and Morocco, helped tremendously. Vietnam's growth was almost certainly because of accelerated 5G deployments and infrastructure and investments being made there. Also, they did say that in Latin America, sales were lower, mostly because of continued price competition there. And on top of this, the company continues to benefit from stability in North America and what they called 'healthy investment levels.'

One thing that I can really appreciate about this picture is that, even though revenue for Networks contracted, profit margins improved nicely. For the final quarter, the 49% gross profit margin exceeded the 47.7% profit margin reported the previous year. Meanwhile, the EBITDA margin for the company expanded from 19.8% to 21.1%. Management has been working hard on cost cutting initiatives and on boosting operational efficiencies. And according to the company, those more than offset a negative impact from a change in product mix. Similarly, as the chart covering these financial results, as I already showed, demonstrates, the expansion of the company’s EBITDA margin can be chalked up to those same improvements and positive foreign currency fluctuations.

The other pain point for the company ended up being its Enterprise segment, which saw revenue contract from $680 million to $511 million. This part of the company engages in the provision of advanced connectivity solutions that help customers to accelerate their own digitalization efforts. It looks as though both global communications platform revenue and enterprise wireless solutions revenue dropped year over year. Some of the sales decline seems to have been intentional. For instance, some of the revenue drop was driven by the company’s decision to sell off iconectiv in the third quarter of last year in a transaction that brought in about $1 billion. In case you aren't familiar with it, iconectiv operates as a provider of certain portability solutions and of core network and operations management, numbering, and data exchange services. This was a valuable company to the business, with operations serving more than 5,000 customers. But clearly, management did the math and preferred the cash instead.

There were other reasons for the weakness that we saw as well. For instance, global connections platform revenue dropped around 7% year over year. This was a deliberate decision made by management to reduce activities in some countries and to lower sales when it came to their legacy solutions. The legacy solutions angle is particularly interesting. In all likelihood, these are lower margin revenue streams. So you would expect to see some profitability improvement, at least from a margin perspective, from this maneuver. On top of this, the company experienced some weakness when it came to its enterprise wireless solutions business because of a reduction in demand associated with private 5G solutions that management chalked up to strong deliveries that occurred the previous year that were not repeated in 2025. Unfortunately, profitability for the segment followed revenue lower in this case, with profit margins declining measurably.

The only bright spot that we really had when it came to revenue ended up being its Cloud Software and Services segment. This part of the company offers up solutions that help providers to move into a more cloud-based system of operating. One such example of what the company provides here would be its Core Networks operation, which helps to transfer network traffic at high speeds. Revenue under this segment actually grew from $2.17 billion to $2.24 billion. This helped to push profits and margins up, as the chart above illustrates.

Based on what management said, the company benefited from growth across all market areas. But most notably, we had its North East Asia and South East Asia, Oceania, and India regions. In these areas, the timing of project deliverables proved immensely important. It seems as though 5G core solutions are proving to be particularly popular, which makes sense considering the global rollout of 5G that's occurring. And from a margin perspective, the expansion of the company saw was driven by a greater share of software sales, which have a tendency to be high margin in nature. On top of this, management was able to capture certain operational efficiencies that boosted results.

What I would find to be particularly compelling here is the overall improvement in profitability that the company exhibited for 2025 in its entirety. This helped to keep the company attractively priced relative to the results achieved. In the chart above, for instance, you can see how the company is priced using results from the last two fiscal years. What we see here is a business that's getting cheaper, even as its share price is on the rise. These are very appealing multiples if you ask me, especially when you consider that the business has a rock-solid balance sheet. The firm has a net cash position as opposed to a net debt position, with that net cash amounting to roughly $2.59 billion. That certainly gives it plenty of wiggle room and fuel with which to grow.

What's really impressive is the fact that management has maintained this solid balance sheet position even after the company has already invested a tremendous amount of capital in various initiatives. This includes research and development costs that came in at around $5 billion for the year, a continued dividend being paid out by the company, and around $4.6 billion of share buybacks that were supported only slightly, to the tune of about $1.1 billion, from asset sales.

Moving forward, there are certain challenges that need to be considered. For instance, the company said that it expects the RAN market for 2026 to be ‘flattish,’ even though the company expects growth in new areas. Even so, the company did say that both the Networks and Cloud Software & Services segment should experience sales growth in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time of the previous year, though the latter will see growth that is lower than the three-year average on a seasonally adjusted basis. Through these trying times, management is going to continue pushing for operational efficiencies; it is investing in technology, including AI. This makes a lot of sense. And hopefully, it will continue to help boost margins moving forward.

Takeaway

On the whole, I would say that Ericsson is doing a fine job right now. Even though revenue dropped year over year, pretty much all the other metrics for the company came in strong. Net profits and almost all of the cash flow metrics improved, driven by operational efficiencies that management is implementing. The company has a solid balance sheet, and the stock is attractively priced. When you add all of this together, it makes sense to maintain it as a ‘buy’ candidate.

data.title: Ring Energy: Solid Debt Reduction Progress In H2 2025
data.description:
Ring Energy (REI) appears to be progressing decently in terms of debt reduction. It paid down $20 million in debt in Q3 2025 and expects to reduce its debt by another $10 million in Q4 2025 despite having a $10 million deferred payment that was due in the quarter.

If Ring goes with a maintenance capex budget in 2026, it would be projected to end 2026 with approximately $375 million in credit facility debt. Ring has also continued to make strides with its cost control efforts, with its Q3 2025 lease operating expense coming in lower than expected and resulting in a $0.25 per BOE reduction to its Q4 2025 guidance for that item.

I am maintaining my estimate of Ring's value at $1.45 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil environment. This also assumes the current strip (at close to $60 WTI oil) for 2026.

Results For Q3 2025

Ring ended up selling an average of 13,332 barrels of oil per day in Q3 2025, which was an 8% decrease compared to Q2 2025, although essentially at the midpoint of its guidance for the quarter.

Ring sold an average of 20,789 BOEPD during Q3 2025, which was 2% lower than its Q2 2025 sales volumes and 3% above the midpoint of its Q3 2025 guidance.

Ring realized slightly negative revenue (negative $0.3 million) from its non-oil sales in Q3 2025, so oil volumes continue to be the thing to focus on.

Ring's lease operating expense averaged $10.73 per BOE for Q3 2025, which was 7% below the midpoint of its guidance and also below the low end of its guidance. This was partially due to the outperformance of Ring's non-oil volumes, although Ring's lease operating expense per barrel of oil sales was also around 4% below the midpoint of its guidance.

Those cost reductions helped Ring generate $13.9 million in adjusted free cash flow during the quarter, and it also paid down $20 million in debt ($2 million more than its prior guidance).

Q4 2025 Expectations

Ring made some minor changes to its guidance for Q4 2025. It kept the midpoint of its oil sales volume expectations the same, but increased its total sales volume guidance by 100 BOEPD at the guidance midpoint. It also appears that around $2 million in capex was shifted from Q3 2025 to Q4 2025.

Ring also reduced its expectations around LOE by $0.25 per BOE. It may generate around $10 million to $11 million in Q4 2025 free cash flow, which is slightly lower than Q3 2025 levels due to lower oil prices as well as slightly reduced sales volumes.

Ring expects to pay down its debt by $10 million in Q4 2025 despite a deferred $10 million payment for its Lime Rock acquisition that was due during the quarter. That would reduce Ring's credit facility borrowings to $418 million at the end of the year.

This is lower than the $433 million year-end borrowing number that I had projected before, but that difference appears to be due to working capital changes. Ring's 2H 2025 free cash flow is tracking in-line with my expectations.

Debt And Divestitures

Ring mentioned that it has a small package of non-operated working interests that it is considering selling. That could add modestly to its debt reduction efforts, although it involves less than 200 BOEPD in production.

Ring's credit facility borrowing base was recently reaffirmed at $585 million. At $60 WTI oil in 2026, Ring expects to generate a bit over $40 million in adjusted free cash flow, which would bring its credit facility debt down to approximately $375 million at the end of 2026. This assumes no working capital changes or additional acquisitions and doesn't include the potential debt paydown from its non-operated working interest sale.

Notes On Valuation

I am keeping my estimate of Ring's value at $1.45 per share in the long term (after 2026) with $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Ring's 2H 2025 free cash flow appears to be tracking to my prior expectations. It also has been doing fairly well in terms of cost controls, but oil prices will mainly determine its free cash flow.

At the current strip in 2026, Ring should be able to continue reducing its debt at a reasonable pace, although it could use $70s oil to significantly accelerate its debt paydown. The difference between $60 and $70 oil is around $35 million in free cash flow in 2026 including hedges.

Ring may be able to generate around $85 million in free cash flow (without hedges) in a $70 WTI oil and maintenance capex scenario going forward.

Conclusion

Ring Energy reported Q3 2025 oil sales volumes in line with expectations and has maintained its oil sales volume guidance for Q4 2025. It has reduced its lease operating costs, doing better than guidance in Q3 2025 and lowering its Q4 2025 guidance by $0.25 per BOE.

Ring is making decent progress paying down its debt, and expects to reduce its debt by $30 million in 2H 2025 despite a $10 million deferred payment. It may be able to further reduce its credit facility debt to around $375 million by the end of 2026 if oil averages $60 this year.

I am maintaining my estimate of Ring's value at $1.45 per share and believe that continued debt reduction is important to being able to realize that value.

data.title: Domino's Pizza Is Now A Fresh Buy After The Recent Dip (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
There have been some interesting changes in the months that followed my previous coverage of Domino’s Pizza, Inc. (DPZ). Inflationary headwinds have remained intense, which caused uncertainty in the restaurant industry. It’s no wonder that the price has stayed flat, which justifies my hold rating from before. On the bright side, it continues to fight back with its operational efficiencies and strong delivery arm. Valuation also shows that DPZ is still reasonable at this level. Meanwhile, technicals are still a bit bearish with some downside risks, but recent overselling opens new buying opportunities.

Why I Love Domino’s Pizza

If I’m being subjective, I will outright say that Domino’s is my favorite pizza brand. It’s not as prolific as many local brands in our country, but it’s on another level if you ask me. It offers great varieties of flavors. On top of that, it remains very generous with weekly deals and promos. But as an investor and analyst, there are plenty of things to like about it.

1. Its Strategic Business Model And Operational Efficiencies Continue To Pay Off. I bet we all know that DPZ has capitalized on good technologies to improve its day-to-day operations, especially its delivery arm, over the years. It has become more effective since the pandemic as consumer behavior evolved along with the digital revolution. As more orders came through its app and/or website, its concern about route optimization, user experience, and brand/network reach continued to pay off. This has also helped DPZ manage its supply chain efficiency in recent years.

To further enhance this platform, DPZ continues to partner with big delivery platforms, such as DoorDash (DASH) and Uber Eats (UBER). Here in our region, DPZ partners with Grab (GRAB) and Food Panda. I’m sure you know that DPZ used to refuse to partner with these apps. But thankfully, it seems that DPZ immediately realized the potential to expand their delivery reach and aggregate restaurants and their menus.

In Southeast Asia, GRAB comprises a huge portion of the total market share. For instance, it holds 91% of market share in the Philippines, while 97% in Malaysia. Obviously, this can take away a portion of DPZ’s delivery demand/sales. This is where it becomes more interesting. DPZ is listed on GRAB. But once you place your order, it will be redirected to your chosen DPZ branch or location. So, its own riders will deliver your order, not GRAB’s. On the other hand, GRAB sets a markup price as a commission or payment for including DPZ in the app. If not, the app will just set a markup price, but the whole delivery fee goes to DPZ. In short, it leverages third-party delivery apps to earn delivery fees.

2. Its Operations Are Still Fruitful Despite Its Continued Expansion. DPZ stays ahead of the competition with its operational strategies and continued expansion. At first glance, this can be risky since stores can predate one another. Also, it may incur more costs amid stubborn inflation. However, DPZ keeps proving that it knows what it’s doing. As you can see, its global retail sales in Q3 2025 reached $4.696B, up by 7% YoY from $4.392B. And if you look at its sales per store, DPZ generated an average amount of $215,908 versus $209,122. So, its expansion still makes sense.

DPZ Global Retail Sales Restaurant Count Average Sales Per Restaurant

Q3 2024

 $4.392B 21,002 $209,122
Q3 2025 $4.696B 21,750 $215,908

It also capitalizes on its well-diversified business model. Its total revenues reached $1.15B, up by 6.2% YoY from $1.08B. Supply chain revenues remained the core driver as they comprised 61% of the total revenue and rose by 7.0% YoY. This proves that its delivery arm remains well-positioned. Its franchising segment is also still strong considering the amount of royalties and advertising it generated last quarter.

More importantly, its cost of sales remained manageable. It only increased by 4.7%, so revenue growth easily offset it. In addition, its percentage of the total operating costs and expenses decreased a bit to 74.4% ($687.2M/($687.2M+$105.1M+$131.6M)) versus 74.5% YoY. This means that DPZ was able to stabilize its cost-sensitivity despite its expansion amid stubborn inflation. As a result, its operating margin rose to 19.5% from 18.4% YoY.

3. DPZ Remains Cheap. DPZ is generous to customers and investors. Aside from its deals, it also stays consistent with its dividend payout. On a yearly basis, dividends amounted to $6.56 in 2025 with a yield of 1.74%. This makes DPZ very attractive to me. So even if the price remains above $400, DPZ still appears affordable.

To reassess this, I updated my Dividend Discount Model. Because its annualized dividend remains the same, my estimated dividend growth rate also remains unchanged. Meanwhile, the risk premium is still 7.0%. But note that the risk-free rate is now 4.3% based on the 10-year treasury yield. Lastly, beta decreased to 1.24, which suggests that DPZ became less volatile than in my previous coverage. That makes sense considering its low price change from my previous coverage. Using all these numbers, my new TP or target price, is now $451.95. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $429.35. This is lower than my previous TP. Nonetheless, it is still higher than the current price, which proves its cheapness.

I also updated my estimation using price ratios. DPZ appears to be trading at a cheap level relative to previous years. For instance, P/E shows that it only trades at 23.66x its EPS versus the five-year average of 28.99x. In addition, it is the cheapest multiple since 2020. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $496.54. Even if we add a 10% risk consideration, we will still get a reasonable TP of $446.89, which is very close to my TP using the Dividend Discount Model. The same applies to P/S, which suggests a TP of $467.32.

4. Liquidity Is Still Decent. I used to be concerned about its liquidity because its debt is high relative to its cash. In fact, the Net Debt/EBITDA is quite high at 4.7x, which means that DPZ needs 4.7 years to repay the whole amount at once. Despite this, I should know that $4.81B or 99.8% of the total amount, will not mature within a year. Also, the maturity profile of its borrowings is also in line with its capacity to repay on time. Note that over 40% of the total borrowings have a maturity of more than five years. Hence, it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Risks

Inflation remains a challenge for DPZ despite its highly impressive performance and unparalleled operational efficiency in the pizza market. At 2.7%, it is still close to the 14-month high of 3.0%. This might be exacerbated by new tariffs since these can raise domestic production costs. As a result, purchasing power and discretionary spending may be affected.

Mitigants

DPZ may indirectly benefit from the policy easing cycle by the Fed. In a short period, the interest rate has already been reduced by 75 bps. Its borrowings won’t be affected immediately since these have fixed rates. But the interest rate cut can boost economic activity, which can also help improve employment and wages to increase purchasing power. In addition, the lower interest rate can reduce borrowing costs, so credit cards and personal loans may become more affordable and accessible. This can support discretionary spending, which can have some positive spillovers in DPZ’s sales. Lastly, it can increase borrower and investor confidence, which can lead to a potential increase in franchisees.

Technicals

DPZ’s technicals are still bearish because the rebound attempt last month did not break above the resistance. It even led to a bull trap due to a dip from $438 to $387. Investors must still be careful because the dip broke below the $398 support. This suggests that the downtrend is not yet over. Also, the price trend is below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. However, the 50-day SMA appears to be shifting its direction. After its downtrend, it flattened before slightly increasing. This tells us that market sentiments have started to become a bit more optimistic. Perhaps buyers saw the recent dip as an opportunity to enter at a discount. After all, its valuation remains cheap.

Meanwhile, MACD and the signal line show new buying opportunities. The MACD line is still trending below the signal line as selling volume keeps increasing. The histogram is also within negative territory, which implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. On a lighter note, we can see that the histogram is moving upwards close to zero or neutral. This suggests that the gap between selling and buying volume is starting to decrease or narrow. We can see new buyers after the recent price drop. In addition, its RSI is increasing again after the stock became oversold. Now, it is still far below the overbought region, so there is enough buying room.

Conclusion

Domino’s Pizza, Inc. remains a very attractive stock with its robust performance, operational efficiencies, strategic expansion, and solid delivery arm improvements. Liquidity remains decent, which can support its continued store openings as pizza demand remains strong. In addition, valuation remains attractive as the stock remains low with high dividends. Technicals are still bearish with some downside risks, but buying opportunities are evident today. I’m upgrading my rating from hold to buy.
